coredata.dc:title: Efficient determination of doxorubicin in serum based on novel Sulfur-Enrichment and Nitrogen-Deficiency carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Wen-Juan
Liu, Kai-Qi
Chen, Jia-Lou
Wang, Gang
Li, Feng
Wang, Yu-Qin
Kong, Fen-Ying
Wang, Wei
Wang, Zhong-Xia
coredata.dc:description: Cancer is currently one of the deadliest diseases in the world. As one of the most common anti-cancer drugs, monitoring the amount of doxorubicin (Dox) with high sensitivity in serum can not only guide the dosage of Dox and minimize their toxicity, but also guarantee the effectiveness of treatment and the safety of patient’s life. Here, a green luminescent probe of the sulfur-enrichment, nitrogen-deficiency carbon nanodots (SCDs) was synthesized by melamine and 3-mercaptopropionic acid as nitrogen-sulfur-oxygen sources. The optical properties and surface functional groups of the SCDs have tremendously changed owing to the enrichment of the sulfur atoms. Interestingly, the SCDs luminescence can be suppressed by Dox through effective inner filter effect, which was permitted to construct high sensing platform for Dox determination. With the help of this mechanism, a wide linear range from 0.1 μM to 300 μM with a low limit of detection of 78.4 nM by utilizing SCDs-based constructed sensing platform were obtained. More importantly, this sensing platform has been successfully used to detect Dox in serum samples, and achieved satisfactory results with the recoveries between 97.7 and 104.2 %. This study conduces to the design of surface sulfur-containing carbon dots and broaden the utilization of functional SCDs in anthraquinone drugs detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfur-enrichment
Carbon nanodots
Inner filter effect
Doxorubicin
Serum sample

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the sulfur-enrichment carbon nanodots
Exploration of optical properties of SCDs
Determination mechanism of SCDs to Dox
Analysis Dox using SCDs as PL probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (A), FT-IR (B), XPS survey spectrum (C) and high-resolution XPS C1s (D), N1s (E), O1s (F) and S2p (G) spectra of SCDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption (curve a), emission (curve b) and excitation (curve c) spectra of the SCDs. (B) PL behaviors of the SCDs at different excitation wavelengths. (C) PL intensities at 450 nm of the SCDs at various pH values and (D) PL intensities of the SCDs in the presence of different concentrations of NaCl.

PL (A) and UV–vis absorption (B) spectra of pure Dox, single SCDs and the SCDs/Dox mixture. (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the Dox (curve a), and the excitation (curve b) and emission (curve c) spectra of the SCDs. (D) The relative PL intensity of SCDs at 450 nm wavelength in the presence of 100 μM Dox at various pH values.

(A) PL spectra of SCDs in the presence of different concentrations of Dox (0 to 300 μM); (B) Linear relationship between [(PL0-PL)/PL0] and CDox. (C) The crossover experiments of the PL assay for Dox over various competitive analytes. The Dox concentration was 100 μM, and the concentrations of other competitive analytes were all 300 μM.
Schematic diagram of the principle of the PL determination of Dox by SCDs.
Comparison of analytical technology, material, linear range and detect limit for Dox analysis.
Determination of Dox in mice serum and BSS.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The data were obtained from three parallel samples.

head.ce:title: Efficient determination of doxorubicin in serum based on novel Sulfur-Enrichment and Nitrogen-Deficiency carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210023, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Wen-Juan Wang and Kai-Qi Liu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cancer is currently one of the deadliest diseases in the world. As one of the most common anti-cancer drugs, monitoring the amount of doxorubicin (Dox) with high sensitivity in serum can not only guide the dosage of Dox and minimize their toxicity, but also guarantee the effectiveness of treatment and the safety of patient’s life. Here, a green luminescent probe of the sulfur-enrichment, nitrogen-deficiency carbon nanodots (SCDs) was synthesized by melamine and 3-mercaptopropionic acid as nitrogen-sulfur-oxygen sources. The optical properties and surface functional groups of the SCDs have tremendously changed owing to the enrichment of the sulfur atoms. Interestingly, the SCDs luminescence can be suppressed by Dox through effective inner filter effect, which was permitted to construct high sensing platform for Dox determination. With the help of this mechanism, a wide linear range from 0.1 μM to 300 μM with a low limit of detection of 78.4 nM by utilizing SCDs-based constructed sensing platform were obtained. More importantly, this sensing platform has been successfully used to detect Dox in serum samples, and achieved satisfactory results with the recoveries between 97.7 and 104.2 %. This study conduces to the design of surface sulfur-containing carbon dots and broaden the utilization of functional SCDs in anthraquinone drugs detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Doxorubicin (Dox, C27H29NO11), an anthracycline, is commonly used as a broad-spectrum anticancer drug to treat various human malignancies, such as breast cancer, hematological malignancies, leukemia sarcoma, lung cancer, lymphoma, malignant melanoma and other tumor cells [1–3]. However, excessive consumption of Dox can cause a variety of side effects including cardiotoxicity, massive alopecia, severe gastrointestinal disorder and inhibition of bone marrow hematopoiesis [4,5]. Comply with the recommendation of the Food and Drug Administration of the United States, the acceptable daily intake of Dox should be 30 mg/m2, and administered continuously for 21 days [6]. Therefore, monitoring and evaluating Dox levels in serum samples is crucial for guaranteeing human health. Compared with the traditional detection platforms [7–10], fluorescent sensing platforms have attracted more attentions from researchers due to their obvious advantages such as excellent selectivity and sensitivity, low invasiveness, fast response, good portability, simple operation process and do not destroy the sample.

Fluorescent carbon nanodots (FCDs) have been widely used in the design of fluorescence-based sensing platforms due to their excellent optoelectronic properties [11]. Beyond the type of doped elements, note that the quantity of doped elements is also one of the key factors on their optical properties of FCDs [12]. Combined with recent development involving doping FCDs with different amounts of nitrogen for the detection of different analytes [12–15], which inspired our curiosity to explore the corresponding optical property and application of its counterpart by doping FCDs with high quantity sulfur. Although many achievements have been made in related researches of sulfur-doped FCDs [16–18], the preparation of sulfur-enrichment FCDs by the polymerization reaction between different precursors has not been reported so far.

Herein, we first prepared the sulfur-enrichment, low-nitrogen carbon nanodots (SCDs) through a facile one-pot hydrothermal process using melamine and 3-mercaptopropionic acid as nitrogen-sulfur-oxygen sources. Wherein sulfur atom content is as high as 13.99 At%. Interestingly, it was found that the emission of SCDs can be effectively suppressed by Dox, the quenching mechanism of SCDs by Dox was then systematically studied, and the inner filter effect (IFE) was identified to responsible for the quenching behavior. Under the optimized conditions, luminescent suppression that is linearly related to the concentration of Dox in the range of 0.1–––300 μM, with a detection limit of 78.4 nM. The mechanism of the constructed Dox detection concept is shown in Scheme 1 . This work further confirms the existence of significant relationships between the quantity of doped element and their optical and physicochemical properties of probes.

Melamine, 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), histidine (His), cysteine (Cys), lactose (Lac), proline (Pro) and ascorbic acid (Asa) were provided from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Doxorubicin hydrochloride (Dox) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Tryptophan (Try), tyrosine (Tyr), glucose (Glu) serine (Ser) and phenylalanine (Phe) were provided from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum sample (BSS) was purchased from Shanghai Sangon Biotech Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Mice serum was obtained from State Key Laboratory of Life Analytical Chemistry, Nanjing University. Ultrapure water was used as the solvent throughout the whole experimental process.

A FP-6500 fluorescent spectrofluorometer (Jasco, Japan) was used to test the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of SCDs, and the Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) was used to analyze the UV–vis absorption spectra. FT-IR spectrum of SCDs was obtained by a Nicolet 5700-IR spectrometer (USA). The elemental content and composition of SCDs were characterized by a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image was performed on a JEM-2100 TEM. (JEOL Ltd., Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the sulfur-enrichment carbon nanodots
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, 50 mg of melamine and 100 μL MPA stock solution were dispersed in 5 mL ethanol. After ultrasonic treatment for 30 min, ∼5.0 mL of the above mixed solution was transferred into a 25 mL autoclave, and heated at 180 °C for 16 h. When the temperature naturally cooling to room temperature, centrifuging the resulting solution at 12,000 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant, i.e., sulfur-enrichment carbon nanodots (SCDs), was carefully collected for later analysis.

To obtain the most ideal Dox detection result, the pH value of the detection system was optimized as follows: the SCDs stock solution (8.0 mg mL−1, 5 μL), Dox (1 mM, 25 μL) and PBS buffer of various pH values (50 mM, 50 μL) were successively added in a centrifuge tube (2 mL). Next, the final volume was set with ultrapure water to 500 μL. After 20 min of standing reaction, the PL spectra were measured at 370 nm excitation, and the slit widths of excitation and emission were both 3.0 nm.

For the determination of Dox, the SCDs stock solution (8.0 mg mL−1, 5 μL) and PBS buffer (pH 7.0, 50 mM, 50 μL) were successively added in a centrifuge tube (2 mL). Next, different concentrations of Dox solutions (0–––300 μM) were separately added to the above solution. Then, the final volume was determined with ultrapure water to 500 μL. After 20 min of standing reaction, the PL spectra were measured using the above conditions. The selectivity of the SCDs-based sensor was evaluated from the PL spectra obtained by replacing Dox with competitive substances or coexisting Dox and interferents under the above conditions.

To verify the practicality of our method, the real samples of mice serum and BBS were selected for analysis. Before mice serum and BBS samples analysis, mice serum and BSS were firstly diluted 50-fold with a PBS buffer (pH 7.0, 10.0 mM), respectively. Briefly, the SCDs stock solution (8.0 mg mL−1, 5 μL) was added to the above diluted mice serum and BSS (pH 7.0, 250 μL). Then, different quantities of Dox standard samples were added to each of the above-mixed solutions to produce 0.5, 1.0 and 5.0 μM Dox in mice serum samples, 10, 50 and 100 μM Dox in BSS samples, respectively. Finally, the final volume of the mixture was set with ultrapure water to 500 μL. The mixture containing 1 % mice serum or BSS was thoroughly mixed to form a uniform solution and incubated at room temperature for 20 min. The PL spectra were recorded under the above conditions.

SCDs were firstly prepared in an ethanol solution employing melamine and MPA as nitrogen-sulfur-oxygen sources, via a facile one-pot hydrothermal procedure (Scheme 1). TEM image in Fig. 1 A shows that SCDs were uniformly distributed nanodots, with the average particle size of ∼ 8.0 nm (inset of Fig. 1A). To investigate the surface composition of SCDs, FT-IR characterization was performed. As shown in Fig. 1B, absorption bands at 3438 cm−1 corresponds to ν(O-H)/ν(N-H), at 2981 and 2930 cm−1 to ν(C-H), at 1732 and 1372 cm−1 to ν(C = O of carboxyl carbonyl), at 1619 and 1419 cm−1 to ν(C = O of amide carbonyl), at 1550 cm−1 to ν(C = N of aromatic heterocycles), at 1341 cm−1 to ν(C-C), at 1241 cm−1 to ν(C-N), at 1180 cm−1 to ν(aromatic C-O), and at 1098 cm−1 to ν(C-O) [19,20]. In addition, FT-IR analysis also confirms the existence of S = O (1032 cm−1) and C-S (665 cm−1) chemical bands and/or functional groups in SCDs surface [17,20]. Next, the elemental content and composition of the SCDs was analyzed by XPS. As shown in Fig. 1C, the full XPS spectrum of SCDs presents five peaks at 532.2, 389.4, 284.2, 226.5 and 161.8 eV, which are ascribed to O1s, N1s, C1s, S2s and S2p, respectively [16,20], with atomic ratios of 10.27 %, 12.24 %, 63.50 % and 13.99 % (inset of Fig. 1C). From the XPS result, the amount of doped sulfur (13.99 At%) in this work is nearly twice higher than in recently reported S-containing carbon dots (7.2 At%) [21], which might bring unexpected performance of carbon nanomaterials. The C1s spectrum exhibits five peaks at 284.1 eV for C-C/C = C, 284.8 eV for C-N, 285.6 eV for C-O/C-S, 288.1 eV for C = O/C = N and 289.4 eV for O-C = O (Fig. 1D) [22,23]. The N1s band unveils pyridinic, graphitic and amine N doping at 398.4, 399.5 and 400.3/401.8 eV, respectively (Fig. 1E) [12], whereas the O1s spectrum reveals the existence of C = O (530.8 eV), C-OH (531.5 eV), C-O-C (532.3 eV), and O-C = O/S = O (533.4 eV) groups in the surface of the SCDs (Fig. 1F) [18,24]. And the S2p band contains three peaks at 162.8, 163.8 and 167.5 eV, which corresponds to S2p3/2C-S, S2p1/2C-S and S = O groups, respectively (Fig. 1G) [16,25]. The obtained XPS results were in good agreement with the FT-IR results. From the XPS and FT-IR results, the successful insertion of S and N atoms into the SCDs through condensation and/or polymerization of melamine and MPA in a high-temperature environment, owing to the emergence of new functional groups and the significant changes in atomic ratio of S/N before (1:6) and after (∼1:1) reaction. Accordingly, such high sulfur content within the SCDs inspire us to explore their physicochemical and luminescent properties, which might greatly supplement the performance of existing carbon nanodots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of optical properties of SCDs
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, within three weeks of storage, no obvious PL change is observed for the SCDs in aqueous solution, indicating its good stability. Fig. 2C shows the strong and stable PL of SCDs over a wide range of pH values (1–12). However, when pH is higher than 12, its fluorescent intensity decreases sharply, which might be attributed to the deprotonation of massive –COOH units and N-containing groups on the edge/surface [15]. The PL intensities of SCDs in the presence of different concentrations of NaCl (up to 0.3 M) were investigated in order to explore its PL stability in a high ionic strength environment. As shown in Fig. 2D, negligible variation in PL intensity was observed, showing high stability of the SCDs even at high ionic strength environment. This result suggests that SCDs has great potential in physiological sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination mechanism of SCDs to Dox
ce:section.ce:para: As discussed above, the obtained SCDs are a new kind of promising nanoprobe with excellent PL properties. The PL of the obtained SCDs emits strong and stable 450 nm wavelength under 370 nm excitation (Fig. 2A). Research has found that when Dox was added into the SCDs solution, the degrees of quenching were gradually reduced with increasing Dox concentration (Fig. 3 A), indicating the high sensitivity for Dox using the obtained SCDs. To reveal the mechanism of PL quenching, UV–vis absorption measurements were further investigated. Fig. 3B shows that the adsorption spectra of SCDs upon the addition of different concentration of Dox have no obvious changes, suggesting that no classical Meisenheimer-complexes generated between Dox and SCDs during the analysis process [32,33]. It is inferred that the inner filter effect (IFE) of the Dox on SCDs, attributed to the absorption of excitation photons and/or emission beam by the absorbers [34,35], might be the main responsibility for the substantial PL quenching of SCDs by Dox [36,37]. From Fig. 3C, the SCDs exhibits a strong PL emission in the range of 400 to 550 nm at 370 nm excitation, which overlaps most of the absorption spectrum of Dox from 400 to 550 nm, and the excitation wavelength (λex = 370 nm) also overlaps the absorption band of Dox. These findings suggest that the absorption of the emitting beam and excited photons leads to the formation of an efficient IFE between Dox and SCDs. Moreover, with the increase of Dox concentration, the absorbance of Dox was enhanced, and the incidence of IFE was increased, showing a stronger quenching effect. Considering that SCDs serve as PL probe for analyzing drug molecule in complex environments, the pH value of the analytical solution should be optimized. As shown in Fig. 3D, when Dox was added to the SCDs solution, the relative quenching degrees were negligible increased with increasing pH 6.0–7.0, whereas it presents a cliff like descent when the pH is above 7.0. Given the practical application of the constructed method in real biological samples, the Dox sensing platform was performed in the approached neutral pH 7.0 PBS buffer. Subsequently, the PL interaction between SCDs and Dox was studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis Dox using SCDs as PL probe
ce:section.ce:para: From the above results, our prepared SCDs showed a surprisingly high sensitivity for Dox. We then tested the practicability of quantification using it for Dox analysis. As shown in Fig. 4 A, with the increase of Dox concentration, the PL intensity of SCDs obviously gradually decreases, which is consistent with the result produced by IFE in the previous analysis. [(PL0-PL)/PL0] and the concentration of Dox is plotted, as shown in Fig. 4B (PL0 and PL are the PL intensities of the SCDs in the absence and presence of Dox, respectively). There is a good linear relationship between the two in a range of 0.1–––300 μM, and the linear equation is y = 0.0023x + 0.1395 (R2 = 0.996), so the detection limit is calculated to be 78.4 nM (3σ/k). The comparison with other sensing platforms is shown in Table 1 , which indicates that our constructed sensing platform has a sensitivity comparable to other probes [4,38–41]. After the addition of Dox (100 μM) and biomolecules (His, Cys, Try, Tyr, Lac, Glu, Pro, Asa, Phe and Ser), some metal ions (Mg2+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Cu2+ and Fe2+) or halogen anions (Br- and I-) at the concentration of 300 μM, the relative value [(PL0-PL)/PL0] in the presence of Dox is much higher than that of all other substances (Fig. 4C), indicating that the SCDs-based sensing probe has strong selectivity for Dox. The insensitivity to biomolecules, metal ions and halogen anions might attribute to the differences in absorption bands of analyte and the chemical inertness of SCDs.

In view of the high sensitivity, good specificity and rapid response of SCDs to Dox, this method should be feasible for the direct detection of Dox in actual samples. In order to verify the applicability, the standard addition method was used to detect and analyze Dox in serum samples, and the corresponding results were shown in Table 2 . The recoveries of Dox in mice serum and BSS samples were between 97.7 and 104.2 % with less than 5.0 % RSD, indicating that SCDs nanoprobe has the advantages of simple, rapid and accurate in actual sample analysis.
The real samples were prepared to the linear range of 0.1–––300 µM before determination.

In short, an environmentally friendly PL probe of sulfur-enrichment, low-nitrogen carbon nanodots was synthesized by melamine and MPA as nitrogen-sulfur-oxygen sources, through a facile one-pot hydrothermal process. Owing to the insertion of high sulfur amounts within the SCDs, the surface functional groups and the electronic bandgap-energy of the SCDs were dramatically improved, which was beneficial for constructing highly selective sensing platform for Dox determination through the efficient IFE mechanism. Under optimal optimization conditions, an excellent linear range of 0.1–––300 μM with a low detection limit of 78.4 nM toward Dox was obtained. More importantly, the potential of our constructed Dox-sensor in practical applications has been demonstrated by analyzing Dox in actual serum samples, showing the expansion of PL carbon-based nanoprobes in their application fields. From the above discussions, we conclude that the controlling the ratio of doped elements on the surface of probe plays an important role in regulating the electronic bandgap-energy of materials.

Wen-Juan Wang: Data curation, investigation and writing - original draft. Kai-Qi Liu: Conceptualization and formal analysis. Jia-Lou Chen and Gang Wang: Validation. Feng Li: Resources. Yu-Qin Wang: Supervision and writing - review & editing. Fen-Ying Kong: Methodology. Wei Wang: Funding acquisition, project administration and writing - review & editing. Zhong-Xia Wang: Funding acquisition, supervision, writing - original draft and writing - review & editing.

Wen-Juan Wang: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Kai-Qi Liu: Conceptualization, Formal analysis. Jia-Lou Chen: Validation. Gang Wang: Validation. Feng Li: Resources. Yu-Qin Wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Fen-Ying Kong: Methodology. Wei Wang: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Zhong-Xia Wang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21876144), Jiangsu Province for the College Graduate Research and Innovation Projects (KYCX23_3470), and the Funding for School-Level Research Projects of Yancheng Institute of Technology (xjr2022012).
